title: Fundamental Groups of Algebraic Varieties and the Shafarevich Conjecture
speaker: Benjamin Thomas Bakker
abstract:
The fundamental group π1(X) is an important invariant of a complex algebraic variety X.  Despite its topological nature, it is closely connected to the geometry of many algebraic structures on X.  In this talk I want to discuss two elementary questions involving the fundamental group:
(1) How many simply-connected algebraic subvarieties does X contain?
(2) How many global holomorphic functions does the universal cover X̃ 
 have?
The second question is related to a famous conjecture of Shafarevich, which asserts that in the case X is a smooth projective variety, X̃ 
 has enough global holomorphic functions to separate points up to compact ambiguity.  I will describe joint work with Brunebarbe and Tsimerman which gives a complete answer to questions (1) and (2) provided π1(X)
 admits a faithful linear representation, using techniques coming from non-abelian Hodge theory.

proof_stages.label: Topology, analytification, and the two guiding questions
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a normal complex algebraic variety, views it as analytic and topological, and formulates the two central questions: how many holomorphic functions live on the universal cover and how algebraic subvarieties with small fundamental group are organized.

proof_stages.label: Curve evidence and holomorphic convexity
proof_stages.summary: The one-dimensional classification of universal covers motivates Shafarevich's conjecture that the universal cover of a smooth compact algebraic variety should be holomorphically convex, meaning it maps properly to a Stein space.

proof_stages.label: Shafarevich map and finite-image subvarieties
proof_stages.summary: Assuming holomorphic convexity, quotienting the Remmert reduction by the fundamental group produces an algebraic map whose fibers are precisely the maximal subvarieties with finite image in the ambient fundamental group.

proof_stages.label: Period-map model from geometric representations
proof_stages.summary: Representations coming from algebraic families yield period maps, whose algebraic images have the same Shafarevich-map behavior: a subvariety is contracted exactly when the associated monodromy has finite image, and the relevant covering of the image is Stein.

proof_stages.label: Representation-theoretic Shafarevich reduction
proof_stages.summary: The main theorems assert that any complex representation factors through a unique algebraic target carrying a large nonextendable representation, and that such a target has Stein universal cover, giving nonconstant holomorphic functions and quasi-holomorphic convexity for faithful linear representations.

proof_stages.label: Archimedean harmonic-map input
proof_stages.summary: Nonabelian Hodge theory at the Archimedean place supplies harmonic metrics and deforms semisimple representations to period-map-like objects, which contract exactly the loci where the representation has compact, equivalently unitary, image.

proof_stages.label: Non-Archimedean reduction input
proof_stages.summary: For p-adic representations, harmonic maps to Bruhat-Tits buildings yield algebraic reductions that contract precisely the subvarieties on which the representation has compact p-adic image, i.e. an integral structure; combined with unitarity this detects finite monodromy.

proof_stages.label: Assembly via definability and density
proof_stages.summary: The Archimedean and non-Archimedean reductions are combined using o-minimal definability and density of p-adic and variation-of-Hodge-structure points, including formal thickenings, to produce the algebraic map required in the main theorems.

examples.name: Rational curves on surfaces and K3 contrast
examples.note: The surface case illustrates the finite-image-subvariety boundedness by saying that rational curves would either form a fibration or be finite, while K3 surfaces show that such a dichotomy is not automatic without the relevant fundamental-group hypothesis.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the general Shafarevich-type picture to a smooth projective surface to derive the dichotomy for genus-zero curves, then contrasts it with the stated K3 behavior

passes.reason: K3 surfaces are cited to show the proposed rational-curve dichotomy can fail, while K3s form a systematic class rather than a sporadic exception.

examples.name: Punctured affine plane C^2 minus the origin
examples.note: This simply connected open variety is not holomorphically convex, so it explains why the noncompact version of the Shafarevich statement must allow quasi-holomorphic convexity.
